[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Yangxin-renmin-huanyin-ni-0022.jpg/250px-Yangxin-renmin-huanyin-ni-0022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yangxin-renmin-huanyin-ni-0022.jpg)

A road sign shows distance to the "Huangshi urban area" (黄石市区) rather than simply "[Huangshi](/wiki/Huangshi "Huangshi")" (黄石). This is a useful distinction, because the sign is located *already* within Huangshi prefectural level city (immediately upon entering its [Yangxin County](/wiki/Yangxin_County,_Hubei "Yangxin County, Hubei") from the neighboring [Xianning](/wiki/Xianning "Xianning")), but still 100 kilometres (62 mi) from the Huangshi main urban area.

A **prefecture-level city** ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 地级市; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 地級市; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Dìjíshì*) or **prefectural city** is an administrative division of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") (PRC), ranking below a [province](/wiki/Province_of_China "Province of China") and above a [county](/wiki/Counties_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Counties of the People's Republic of China") in China's administrative structure. It usually consists of multiple sub-prefecture-level cities or towns and the surrounding countryside.

## Details

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prefecture-level_city&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Details")]

During the Republican era, many of China's prefectural cities were designated as **[counties](/wiki/County_(China) "County (China)")** as the country's second level division below a province. From 1949 to 1983, the official term was a **province-administrated city** (Chinese: 省辖市). Prefectural level cities form the second level of the administrative structure (alongside [prefectures](/wiki/Prefecture_of_China "Prefecture of China"), [leagues](/wiki/Leagues_of_China "Leagues of China") and [autonomous prefectures](/wiki/Autonomous_prefecture "Autonomous prefecture")). Administrative chiefs (mayors) of prefectural level cities generally have the same rank as a division chief (Chinese: 司长) of a national ministry. Since the 1980s, most former prefectures have been renamed into prefecture-level cities.

A prefectural city is a "city" (Chinese: 市; pinyin: *shì*) and a "prefecture" (Chinese: 地区; pinyin: *dìqū*) that have been merged into one consolidated and unified jurisdiction. As such it is simultaneously a city, which is a municipal entry with subordinate districts, and a prefecture, with subordinate county-level cities and counties, as well as an administrative division of a province.

A prefectural city is often not a "city" in the literal sense of the term (i.e., a large continuous urban settlement), but instead an administrative unit comprising, typically, a main central urban area (the core city, city as in the usual sense, usually but not always with the same name as the prefectural level city) surrounded by [rural](/wiki/Rural "Rural") areas or small towns, which together are divided into districts, and some surrounding counties or county-level cities governed by the prefecture-level city on behalf of the province, which all have their own urban areas surrounded by their own rural areas. The urban areas of the surrounding counties are usually smaller than the core urban area, and towns also form small urban areas scattered in the rural. The larger prefectural cities span over 100 kilometres (62 mi).

Prefectural cities nearly always contain multiple [counties](/wiki/County_(People%27s_Republic_of_China) "County (People's Republic of China)"), [county level cities](/wiki/County-level_cities "County-level cities"), and other such sub-divisions. This results from the fact that the formerly predominant prefectures, which prefectural cities have mostly replaced, were themselves large administrative units containing cities, smaller towns, and rural areas. To distinguish a prefectural city from its actual urban area (city in the strict sense), the term *shìqū* (市区; "urban area"), is used.

The first prefectural cities were created on 5 November 1983. Over the following two decades, prefectural cities have come to replace the vast majority of Chinese prefectures; the process is still ongoing.

Most provinces are composed entirely or nearly entirely of prefectural cities. Of the 22 provinces and five autonomous regions of the PRC, only nine provinces ([Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan"), [Guizhou](/wiki/Guizhou "Guizhou"), [Qinghai](/wiki/Qinghai "Qinghai"), [Heilongjiang](/wiki/Heilongjiang "Heilongjiang"), [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan"), [Gansu](/wiki/Gansu "Gansu"), [Jilin](/wiki/Jilin "Jilin"), [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei"), and [Hunan](/wiki/Hunan "Hunan")) and three autonomous regions ([Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang"), [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet_Autonomous_Region "Tibet Autonomous Region"), and [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia "Inner Mongolia")) have at least one or more [second level or prefectural level divisions](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China#Prefectural_level "Administrative divisions of the People's Republic of China") that are not prefectural cities.

Criteria that a [prefecture](/wiki/Prefectures_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Prefectures of the People's Republic of China") must meet to become a prefectural level city:

* An urban centre with a non-rural population over 250,000
* gross output of value of industry of 200,000,000 [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") ([US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")32 million)
* the output of the [tertiary sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector "Tertiary sector") supersedes that of the [primary sector](/wiki/Primary_sector "Primary sector"), contributing over 35% of the [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product")[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Fifteen large prefectural level cities have been granted the status of [sub-provincial city](/wiki/Sub-provincial_city "Sub-provincial city"), which gives them much greater autonomy.

[Shijiazhuang](/wiki/Shijiazhuang "Shijiazhuang"), [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou,_Jiangsu "Suzhou, Jiangsu"), and [Zhengzhou](/wiki/Zhengzhou "Zhengzhou") are the largest prefectural level cities with populations approaching or exceeding some sub-provincial cities.

A [sub-prefecture-level city](/wiki/Sub-prefecture-level_city "Sub-prefecture-level city") is a [county-level city](/wiki/County-level_city "County-level city") with powers approaching those of prefectural level cities.

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prefecture-level_city&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classification")]

There are a total of three classifications of prefecture-level cities:

* **Regular prefectural level city**, which consists of [counties](/wiki/CPRC "CPRC"), [county level cities](/wiki/CLPRC "CLPRC"), and [districts](/wiki/District_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "District of the People's Republic of China") subdivisions.
* **Consolidated district-governed prefectural level city**, which only consists of [districts](/wiki/District_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "District of the People's Republic of China") as subdivisions.
  + Currently there are only 13 cities under this classification: [Ezhou](/wiki/Ezhou "Ezhou"), [Foshan](/wiki/Foshan "Foshan"), [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou"), [Haikou](/wiki/Haikou "Haikou"), [Karamay](/wiki/Karamay "Karamay"), [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"), [Sansha](/wiki/Sansha "Sansha"), [Sanya](/wiki/Sanya "Sanya"), [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen"), [Wuhai](/wiki/Wuhai "Wuhai"), [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan "Wuhan"), [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), and [Zhuhai](/wiki/Zhuhai "Zhuhai")
* **Prefectural level city with no county-level divisions (direct-piped city)**, which are cities that do not govern any county-level divisions such as counties, county level cities, or legal administrative districts.
  + Currently there are only four cities under this classification: [Danzhou](/wiki/Danzhou "Danzhou"), [Dongguan](/wiki/Dongguan "Dongguan"), [Jiayuguan](/wiki/Jiayuguan_City "Jiayuguan City"), and [Zhongshan](/wiki/Zhongshan "Zhongshan")

## Cartographic expression and statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prefecture-level_city&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Cartographic expression and statistics")]

In Europe and North America, cities are generally represented as points, while counties are represented as areas. Thus, [Bloomington, Indiana](/wiki/Bloomington,_Indiana "Bloomington, Indiana"), is indicated on the map by a point, which is distinct from, and enclosed by, the area of [Monroe County, Indiana](/wiki/Monroe_County,_Indiana "Monroe County, Indiana"). In China, however, large cities such as Xianning may, in reality, contain both urban and rural elements. Moreover, they may enclose counties or other cities. On a less detailed map, Xianning would be indicated by a point, more or less corresponding to the coordinates of its city government. Other populous areas may also be exhibited as points, such as the county of [Tongshan](/wiki/Tongshan_County "Tongshan County"), with no indication that Tongshan is, in fact, enclosed by Xianning. On a more detailed map, Xianning would be drawn as an area, similar to a county of the United States, and Tongshan would be drawn as a smaller area within Xianning.

This convention may lead to difficulty in the identification of places mentioned in older sources. For example, [Guo Moruo](/wiki/Guo_Moruo "Guo Moruo") writes that he was born in the town of Shawan, within the prefecture of Leshan, and attended primary school in the town of Jiading. A modern map is unlikely to show either town: Shawan, because it is too small, and Jiading, because it is the seat of Leshan, and is therefore indicated on the map by a point labelled "Leshan." A more detailed map would show Shawan as a district within Leshan, but Jiading would still be missing.

Statistics of China such as population and industrial activity are generally reported along prefectural city lines. Thus, the relatively unknown city of [Huangshi](/wiki/Huangshi "Huangshi") has 2.5 million residents, more than most European capitals, but upon closer inspection, the city covers an area almost 100 kilometers across. Furthermore, Huangshi contains several other cities, such as [Daye](/wiki/Daye "Daye"). If a person wished to calculate the population of the urban area of Huangshi, and had a map of Huangshi, and a table of its population by district, the task might not be easy. The urban area might be split between several districts, and some of those districts may include rural elements as well.
